Summary: This study investigates the utility of static and dynamic 18F-DOPA PET radiomics in differentiating high-grade glioma (HGG) progression from treatment-related changes (TRC). The study compares the diagnostic performance of radiomic models, trained on data from one center and validated on data from another, to the current PET imaging standard of care (TBRmean). The results suggest that radiomic features provide only a marginal improvement over the classical TBRmean analysis.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation of healthy brain and tumor VOIs performed by a nuclear physician using LifeX software.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed by a nuclear physician, so it is not fully automated.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts hand-crafted radiomic features from static TBR and dynamic TTP parametric images.

Condition4.reason: The study uses tabular data in the form of extracted radiomic features.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning approach. Instead, it relies on hand-crafted feature extraction and machine learning models.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study defines eligibility criteria for patient selection, including histological confirmation of HGG and a minimum interval between radiation therapy and PET acquisition.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is based on histopathology or clinical-radiology follow-up based on RANO criteria, which is a well-established standard.

Item4.reason: The study involves two different institutions (CHRU of Nancy and Pitié-Salpêtrière hospital in Paris, France) as diagnostic imaging data sources.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is 18F-FDOPA PET, which is a clinically translatable imaging technique for glioma assessment, recommended by RANO.

Item6.reason: The study reports the image acquisition parameters for the different imaging systems used, including voxel sizes, reconstruction algorithms, and post-reconstruction filters.

Item7.reason: The study includes only patients with a minimum 3-month interval between the end of radiation therapy and the 18F-FDOPA PET acquisition to reduce the risk of false positives. The final diagnoses were determined from the histopathology or from the clinical-radiology follow-up during the 6-month follow-up period.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including the use of LifeX software and a semi-automatic segmentation based on a threshold of 1.6 of mean standardized uptake value of healthy brain (SUVmean) to determine tumor VOIs.

Item9.reason: The segmentation method used in the study is semi-automatic, requiring human intervention, rather than fully automated.

Item10.reason: The segmentation was performed by a single nuclear physician (L.R.).

Item11.reason: The study appropriately uses image preprocessing techniques, including resampling to correct for different voxel sizes, registration to correct for patient movements, and denoising using the HYPR-LR method. The parameters for gray level discretization are also described.

Item12.reason: The study uses the pyradiomics package for feature extraction, which is a standardized software. The version is not explicitly mentioned in the main text, but it is available online.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (statistical, histogram-based, local-intensity, and textural features) and provides a link to the pyradiomics package, which contains mathematical justifications of radiomic features.

Item14.reason: The study performs dimensionality reduction using hierarchical clustering based on an absolute spearman correlation coefficient (SCC) as distance matrix and a threshold of 0.9 to remove redundant features.

Item15.reason: The study performs dimensionality reduction using hierarchical clustering based on an absolute spearman correlation coefficient (SCC) as distance matrix and a threshold of 0.9.

Item16.reason: The study uses 85 patients and reduces the number of features through hierarchical clustering. The performance is assessed on an independent test set, suggesting an appropriate balance between dimensionality and data size.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline.

Item18.reason: The study splits the data into training and test sets based on different hospital centers (Nancy and Paris, respectively). All transformations and algorithms were fitted using only the training set and were subsequently applied to the test set.

Item19.reason: The study considers clinical features, such as age, sex, histopathological WHO grade, IDH mutation status, 1p/19q codeletion status, previous tumor resection and contrast enhancement on MRI, and uses ComBat method to harmonize the data.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics, such as AUC, accuracy, precision, F1 score, and balanced accuracy. The 95% confidence intervals (CI) of individual metrics were derived from the distribution of performances obtained with the individual 1000 bootstrapped, trained models.

Item21.reason: The study includes uncertainty measures in the form of 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the performance metrics, derived from the distribution of performances obtained with the individual 1000 bootstrapped, trained models.

Item22.reason: The study does not explicitly assess the calibration of the final models.

Item23.reason: The study compares the performance of models using both static and dynamic PET parameters to a model using only static TBRmean parameters, justifying the use of multi-parametric imaging by comparing their performance.

Item24.reason: The study compares the radiomic models to a reference model based on TBRmean, which is representative of the clinical practice. The statistical significance of the performance differences is evaluated using Wilcoxon tests with multiple comparisons correction.

Item25.reason: The study does not explicitly compare the performance of the radiomic models to a simple baseline reference model, such as a Zero Rules/No Information Rate classifier.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set (Pitié-Salpêtrière hospital patients) that is sampled from the same source as the training set (CHRU of Nancy patients).

Item27.reason: The model is tested with independent data from another institution (Pitié-Salpêtrière hospital).

Item28.reason: The study provides the extracted data in a supplementary table (Table S1).

Item29.reason: The study does not explicitly state that all scripts related to automatic segmentation and/or modeling are shared with the public.

Item30.reason: The study does not make the final model available in the form of a raw model file or as a ready-to-use system.
